topic: The Ancient City That Voted to Destroy Itself

part1.title: The City That Voted to Erase Itself | Part 1
part1.script: One ancient city held a vote — and the majority chose to wipe themselves off the map. Not invaded, not destroyed by fire or plague. They picked up, packed out, and made sure no enemy could ever claim what they'd built. We're talking about Carthage — not the famous destruction Rome inflicted in 146 BC, but the earlier moment when Carthaginians themselves burned their own fleet and leveled their own harbor walls rather than surrender. Some chose death over occupation. Citizens torched homes their grandparents built. The city that once rivaled Rome deleted itself on purpose. Part 2 reveals the one thing they buried underground first — and it survived 2,000 years. Follow so you don't miss Part 2.
part1.search_summary: Carthaginians destroyed their own city and fleet rather than surrender to Rome in 146 BC.
part1.comment_question: Would you burn your own home rather than let an enemy take it?
part1.topic: The Ancient City That Voted to Destroy Itself

part2.title: What Carthage Hid Before It Burned | Part 2
part2.script: Right before Carthage burned, someone hid something. Archaeologists digging beneath modern Tunis in 1994 found a sealed underground deposit — religious objects, gold amulets, infant burial urns, and ritual texts — deliberately entombed below street level while the city above was still standing. The Carthaginians called it the Tophet. They weren't just hiding valuables from Rome; they were preserving their identity underground because they knew the surface was gone. Everything Rome wanted to erase, they buried where Rome wouldn't look. And here's the strange part — Rome actually ordered a total memory wipe. They salted the earth, renamed the territory, and banned any record of Carthaginian culture for over a century. Part 3 reveals how one buried object finally cracked that erasure open — and changed what we know about the ancient world entirely. Follow for the conclusion in Part 3.
part2.search_summary: Archaeologists found Carthaginian ritual deposits buried underground in Tunis before Rome's destruction in 146 BC.
part2.comment_question: Rome erased an entire civilization's records — what else did they delete?
part2.source_note: UNESCO Carthage excavations, 1994; Lawrence Stager, Harvard, 1980s
video_queries: ruins of Carthage Tunisia aerial drone
part2.topic: The Ancient City That Voted to Destroy Itself

part3.title: The Erasure That Backfired on Rome | Part 3
part3.script: Rome didn't just destroy Carthage — it tried to scrub it from human memory. They banned Punic writing, melted coins, and reassigned the land. But that buried Tophet in Tunis held inscriptions in Punic script that survived intact. When scholars finally decoded them in the 19th century, they revealed Carthage had a democratic assembly, a senate, and elected judges called suffetes — centuries before Rome claimed those ideas as their own. Rome may have invented Western civilization's origin story, and it left out the civilization that helped write it. If you watched all three parts — you now know the city Rome feared so much it tried to delete from existence actually shaped the world Rome built. Half the political vocabulary Europe inherited runs through a city Rome pretended never existed. Follow for more series like this.
part3.search_summary: Decoded Punic inscriptions prove Carthage had democratic governance predating Rome's own political institutions.
part3.comment_question: If Rome stole Carthage's ideas, what else in history got credited to the wrong civilization?
part3.source_note: Sabatino Moscati, The World of the Phoenicians, 1968
video_queries: museum Phoenician Punic artifacts close up
part3.topic: The Ancient City That Voted to Destroy Itself
